attributes.stem:
A 71-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of absolute constipation on a background of increasing constipation over the last two months. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, his pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 105/72 mmHg, and temperature 37.8°C. Abdominal examination reveals a distended abdomen with tenderness and guarding in the right iliac fossa. Digital rectal examination shows an empty rectum. A plain abdominal X-ray shows a grossly distended caecum, approximately 14 cm diameter, with distension of the rest of the large bowel down to a cut-off point in the sigmoid colon. No gas is seen beyond this point. No other findings were found on the abdominal X-ray. Intravenous fluids are started.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following interventions is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a large bowel obstruction, from the ileocaecal valve to the sigmoid colon. The most common cause of a large bowel obstruction is malignancy, which in this patient is most likely blocking the sigmoid colon. As small bowel dilatation was not seen on the X-ray, the chance for a 'closed loop' obstruction due to a competent ileocaecal valve (meaning the faecal matter cannot move forward or backward) is high, resulting in immediate danger of ischaemia and perforation. The caecum has reached a danger point, in terms of perforation (13 cm diameter). Additionally, he has localised peritonitis, indicating the strong possibility of an inflammatory process or perforation. This patient has a large bowel obstruction, likely due to malignancy, and is at high risk of perforation. Emergency intervention and laparotomy cannot be delayed.

Of note, in this case, although a CT scan of the chest and abdomen can be utilised to confirm malignancy in this patient, or to confirm the diagnosis of a large bowel obstruction (CT with contrast), an emergency laparotomy is the better plan of immediate management. However, a CT could be utilised if this was not an emergency situation, especially if the patient has to wait for surgical intervention.

If this patient was unsuitable for surgery, non-surgical decompression solutions can be utilised. These include rigid and flexible sigmoidoscopy, and any form of enema. However, if this patient has a sigmoid malignancy stenosing his sigmoid colon, a sigmoidoscopy or enema may be of little use. Flexible sigmoidoscopy is typically utilised to decompress the colon if a patient has sigmoid volvulus, and this is to allow for semi-elective surgery or for high-risk surgical candidates. Rigid sigmoidoscopy is typically not preferred over flexible sigmoidoscopy and therefore is rarely used.

A gastrografin enema is typically utilised prior to X-rays to visualise the large bowel, and can have some therapeutic treatment by assisting in decompressing faecal matter in the distal bowel and rectum. However, a gastrografin enema will not assist this patient in emergently removing the large bowel obstruction and decompressing the caecum, nor for removal of the likely malignancy that would be found.

Nasogastric decompression is useful in small bowel obstruction to decompress the stomach and prevent aspiration. However, it has no role in the management of large bowel obstruction.
learningPoints: Emergency laparotomy is indicated in any patient with a large bowel obstruction with complications (ischemia or perforation) or high risk of acutely developing complications.
A flexible sigmoidoscopy can be utilised to decompress the colon in patients with sigmoid volvulus.
The most common cause of large bowel obstruction is malignancy, with the second most common being sigmoid volvulus.
references: Lieske B, Meseeha M. Large Bowel Obstruction. In: StatPearls [online]. 2021. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441888/

Atamanalp SS, Atamanalp RS. The role of sigmoidoscopy in the diagnosis and treatment of sigmoid volvulus. Pak J Med Sci. 2016;32(1):244-8. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4795878/

Jaffe T, Thompson WM. Large-Bowel Obstruction in the Adult: Classic Radiographic and CT Findings, Etiology, and Mimics. Radiology. 2015;275(3):651-63. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25997131/
